Sending Gmail with nodemailer After Google Killed Password Auth (OAuth 2.0)
This post is pretty old now, so I rewrote a fresh tutorial — you can

(article in Mandarin).
I'm keeping this one around as a learning record

This time I want to add an extra note about nodemailer. Google no longer accepts plain username-and-password auth for sending Gmail, so I'm writing down how to set things up using Express + OAuth 2.0 + nodemailer + Gmail instead.
In the original chapter, the nodemailer setup looked like this:
language-js shiki shiki-themes github-light github-light github-dark-high-contrast
// The account and password used to send mail — you can switch to dotenv later to keep your credentials safe
But these days Google has changed how it authenticates, so it no longer accepts logging in with a username and password. You'll end up with this error:
535-5.7.8 Username and Password not accepted. Learn more at\n535 5.7.8  https://support.google.com/mail/?p=BadCredentials d5sm35056162pfd.107 - gsmtp
nodemailer's own docs also recommend switching to OAuth2, so this post fills in the gap on how to send Gmail through OAuth.
Create a new project in the top left
Name the project whatever you want — I called mine Gmail Test.
Next, open the side menu and you'll see
On the APIs & Services page, click
Once the Gmail API is enabled, go to
At this point Google will probably tell you "To create an OAuth client ID, you must first configure your product name on the consent screen." So click in and set it up. One thing to watch — for
and you can submit (it's just for testing anyway). Don't sweat the rest — just keep hitting save and continue.
Here's an important bit: newer Google OAuth requires you to add test users. So at the
step (step 3), you have to add the account you'll actually test with (a Google account). Make sure this is the exact account you're going to use with Node.js — not some random account you threw in.
If you skip adding a test user, you'll hit this screen later in the flow.
When you choose here, make sure you pick
— not Desktop app, Chrome app, or anything else. If you choose something other than Web application, you won't be able to continue.
" with the trailing slash — that will absolutely break authorization.
Finally you'll get your Client ID and Client Secret. Keep them somewhere safe — you'll need them shortly.
Step 2 — Get the refresh token and access token
. Click the gear icon in the top right, check "Use your own OAuth credentials", and enter the Client ID and Client Secret you just got.
Then in the left-hand input box, enter "
When you click Authorize APIs, you'll be taken to that familiar Google sign-in screen. If you have multiple accounts, pick the right one.
This screen looks a little scary, but don't worry — it's our own app, so it's fine.
This app isn't verified by Google
Google takes user permissions seriously, so it'll ask you one more time.
Yeah... see? Google really does care about your account security.
Now you'll find yourself back at the original screen. Click "Exchange authorization code for tokens".
At this point you'll get both an Access token and a Refresh token. Write both of them down.
Finally, head back to our earlier project. Like I mentioned, the original code logged in with a plain username and password to use nodemailer, so we need to adjust it a bit. Basically, you pass in
via environment variables and change the code to this:
If you're unsure about the env part, you can look back at
Congrats — you've got nodemailer sending mail through OAuth.

This time I want to add an extra note about nodemailer. Google no longer lets you send Gmail messages with just a username and password, so I'm writing down how to set up Express + OAuth 2.0 + nodemailer + Gmail instead. It's a quick walkthrough — building the OAuth client, grabbing the refresh and access tokens, and wiring them into nodemailer.
